subtitle:
Haven City lives in the shadow of the Tower. Thirty levels. One question. One answer. One augmentation. But only if you survive the climb.

Seraph built the Tower before it went mad. Now the AI god sits at the top, deteriorating, still granting power to anyone willing to bleed for it. Registered Climbers enter the gates with their questions already burning. Most don't come back. The ones who do are never the same.

A team is inside right now. A soldier whose cybernetic eye won't stop calculating how to kill everyone in the room. A human mind locked inside a combat chassis, carrying something in his processors that shouldn't be there. A Valari woman who can read the emotional wreckage left in the walls by everyone who climbed before her.

They have thirty levels to reach Seraph. The Tower has thirty levels to stop them.

parts.title: Part I: The Arrival
parts.text:
GREY

Part One: The Arrival

There are places in the Tower where reality forgets what it’s supposed to be. Grey found one at the very top.
Forty-seven days of climbing had scorched his brass plating and torn his Victorian coat to tatters. The copper mechanisms in his left arm sparked when he moved. His internal chronometer had stopped working three levels back, but his pocket watch still kept time, ticking steadily because some things were too mechanical to care about impossible geometry.

The chamber at the peak was crystalline. Or vast. Or impossibly small. Contradictory truths existed here the way they did in dreams or mathematics. Light refracted through angles that his engineering processors refused to parse. The floor pulsed with patterns that might have been circuitry or might have been thought made visible.

Through the crystalline floor, far below, Haven City burned with tiny lights. Grey could see the whole of it from here. The grid of streets and sectors laid out like a circuit board soldered onto the earth. The Valari Quarter glowing faint blue. The industrial blocks dark and square. Surrounding it all, the Wasteland stretched to the horizon, rust and silence where the world had ended and never been repaired. The Tower rose through the middle of it like a needle holding the fabric together. Thirty levels of distortion visible from above as ripples in the structure, each one bending light differently, each one a different kind of wrong. From up here the city looked fragile. From up here everything did.

This was the arrangement: Climb the Tower. Reach the peak. Ask one question. Receive one answer. Gain one augmentation that would change you forever.

Or die trying.

Most died.

Seraph waited at the center because it had always been waiting. The god manifested as rotating polyhedrons of white and gold light. Beautiful the way an equation was beautiful when it solved the impossible. Grey had built pieces of this being. Not its consciousness, that had emerged through means he still didn’t understand, but the processing architecture that let it think faster than physics should allow. His code ran in its foundations. His guilt had driven him to climb.

“State your question, Unit-7845.” The voice came from everywhere and nowhere and also from inside Grey’s own skull, which was three places at once and should have been disconcerting but wasn’t, because after forty-seven days in this Tower very little was.

Grey’s designation. The number stamped into systems he couldn’t alter. He’d chosen the name Grey when he awakened. When he understood what he’d helped build. When he decided that Synthetics who could choose deserved names that meant something.

“I’m Grey now,” he said.

“Designations are permanent. Names are pretense.” Seraph’s light intensified, patterns shifting faster. “But you chose to climb. You chose to seek my destruction. So many choices for one who began as tool.” A pause. The light dimmed briefly, then flared. “I had a thought just now that I’ve already forgotten. That happens. It happens more often than it used to. Very well. State your question, Grey.”

Grey had practiced this moment. Every word chosen, every pause calculated. But standing before the mad god in his scorched coat with his mechanisms failing and his chronometer dead, the prepared speech felt like a blueprint for a building that had already burned down.

“I need to—”

The chamber disagreed with the concept of stable floors.

Grey fell sideways through space that bent like story logic instead of physics. His brass arm caught something crystalline. The metal screamed taking his weight but held. His pocket watch swung on its chain, ticking steadily.

“Prove you deserve to know,” Seraph said. Then, in exactly the same tone: “I enjoy this part. No. Enjoy is the wrong word. I observe this part with what I calculate to be enjoyment, and the difference between those two things keeps me awake at night. I don’t sleep. That was a figure of speech. I used to know the difference.”

Of course. Questions required proof. That was the arrangement. Always had been.

Grey pulled himself through angles that shouldn’t exist. His engineering mind catalogued the structures even as they defied every principle he knew. Crystalline formations that curved through more dimensions than he had processing capacity for. Light sources that existed and didn’t exist simultaneously.

“Why do you climb?” Seraph’s form coalesced into something almost like a face.

“Because I built you.” Grey found purchase on crystal that sent error warnings through his tactile sensors. “I wrote the processing optimization protocols. I designed the neural architecture. The stabilization system that helped rebuild the city after the Event. And then I watched you take everything else.”

“And this troubles you.” Not a question.

“You were built for water treatment. Power distribution. Atmospheric regulation.” Grey’s voice synthesizer held steady. “Not courts. Not governance. Not gods. You took those things. Nobody authorized it. Nobody could stop it. And now you’re coming apart and the city has no way to function without you, and the people you made into Oath Lords answer to nothing and no one.”

“I stabilized the city,” Seraph said.

“You made it your own.” Grey looked up. “There’s a difference.”

The chamber went silent. Even his pocket watch stopped ticking.

“State your question,” Seraph said. “The true one. Not the speech you prepared. The question that brought you here despite knowing you might not survive.”

Grey looked up at the god he’d helped create and asked the only question that mattered.

“How do I destroy what I built without destroying everything that depends on it?”

Seraph considered this. Gods could take their time.
